Annual Exam - 2023

Code: 65/3/1 (set-1)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five Sections - A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A, Questions no. 1 to 18 are multiple choice questions (MCQs) and questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B, Questions no. 21 to 25 are very short answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C, Questions no. 26 to 31 are short answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D, Questions no. 32 to 35 are long answer (LA) type questions carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E, Questions no. 36 to 38 are case study based questions carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section B, 3 questions in Section C, 2 questions in Section D and 2 questions in Section E.
  9. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION - A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 4 & \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{z} & 2 & \mathrm{y} \\ -3 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} is a symmetric matrix, then the value of x + y + z is:

a. 10
b. 6
c. 8
d. 0
2.

If A{tex}\cdot{/tex}(adj A) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then the value of |A| + |adj A| is equal to:

a. 12
b. 9
c. 3
d. 27
3.

A and B are skew-symmetric matrices of same order. AB is symmetric, if:

a. AB = O
b. AB = -BA
c. AB = BA
d. BA = O
4.

For what value of {tex}\text x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}, is A + A' = {tex}\sqrt 3{/tex} I, where A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \text x & \sin \text x \\ -\sin \text x & \cos \text x \end{array}\right]{/tex}?

a. {tex}\frac {\pi}{3}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac {\pi}{6}{/tex}
c. 0
d. {tex}\frac {\pi}{2}{/tex}
5.

Let A be the area of a triangle having vertices (x1, y1), (x2, y2) and (x3, y3). Which of the following is correct?

a. {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{x}_1 & \mathrm{y}_1 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_2 & \mathrm{y}_2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_3 & \mathrm{y}_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|= \pm \mathrm{A}{/tex}
b. {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{x}_1 & \mathrm{y}_1 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_2 & \mathrm{y}_2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_3 & \mathrm{y}_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|= \pm2 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
c. {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{x}_1 & \mathrm{y}_1 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_2 & \mathrm{y}_2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_3 & \mathrm{y}_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|= \pm \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{x}_1 & \mathrm{y}_1 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_2 & \mathrm{y}_2 & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_3 & \mathrm{y}_3 & 1 \end{array}\right|^2{/tex} = A2
6.

{tex}\int 2^{\text x+2}{/tex} dx is equal to:

a. 2x+2 + C
b. 2x+2 log 2 + C
c. {tex}\frac{2^{\mathrm{x}+2}}{\log 2}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
d. {tex}2 \cdot \frac{2^{\mathrm{x}}}{\log 2}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
7.

{tex}\int \frac{2 \cos 2 \text x-1}{1+2 \sin \text x} \text {dx}{/tex} is equal to:

a. x - 2 cos x + C
b. x + 2 cos x + C
c. -x - 2 cos x + C
d. -x + 2 cos x + C
8.

The solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\text {dx}}{\text x}+\frac{\text {dy}}{\text y}{/tex} = 0 is:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{\text x}+\frac{1}{\text y}{/tex} = C
b. log x - log y = C
c. xy = C
d. x + y = C
9.

What is the product of the order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2} \sin \text y+\left(\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}\right)^3{/tex} cos y = {tex}\sqrt {\text y}{/tex}?

a. 3
b. 2
c. 6
d. not defined
10.

If a vector makes an angle of {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} with the positive directions of both x-axis and y-axis, then the angle which it makes with positive z-axis is:

a. {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac {3\pi}{4}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}
d. 0
11.

{tex}\vec {\text a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text b}{/tex} are two non-zero vectors such that the projection of {tex}\vec {\text a}{/tex} on {tex}\vec {\text b}{/tex} is 0. The angle between {tex}\vec {\text a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text b}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}
b. {tex}\pi{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}
d. 0
12.

In {tex}\triangle\text {ABC}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}{/tex} = {tex}\hat {\text i} + \hat {\text j} + 2\hat {\text k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}{/tex} = {tex}3\hat {\text i} - \hat {\text j} + 4\hat {\text k}{/tex}. If D is mid-point of BC, then vector {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}{/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}4 \hat{\text i}+6 \hat{\text k}{/tex}
b. {tex}2 \hat{\text i}-2 \hat{\text j}+2 \hat{\text k}{/tex}
c. {tex}\hat{\text i}-\hat{\text j}+\hat{\text k}{/tex}
d. {tex}2 \hat{\text i}+3 \hat{\text k}{/tex}
13.

The value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the angle between the lines {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\text i}+\hat{\text j}+\hat{\text k}{/tex} + {tex}\mathrm{p}(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = (1 + q){tex}\vec {\text i}{/tex} + (1 + q){tex}\vec {\text j}{/tex} + (1 + q){tex}\vec {\text k}{/tex} is {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} is:

a. -4
b. 4
c. 2
d. -2
14.

If P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac 18{/tex} and {tex}\text P(\bar {\text A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac 34{/tex}, then {tex}\text P\left(\frac {\text B}{\text A}\right){/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac 13{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac 16{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac 23{/tex}
15.

The value of k for which function f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}\text x^2, & \text x \geq 0 \\ \text {kx}, & \text x<0\end{cases}{/tex} is differentiable at x = 0 is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. any real number
d. 0
16.

If y = {tex}\frac{\cos \text x-\sin \text x}{\cos \text x+\sin \text x}{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} is:

a. -sec2{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\text x\right){/tex}
b. sec2{tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\text x\right){/tex}
c. {tex}\log \left|\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\text x\right)\right|{/tex}
d. {tex}-\log \left|\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\text x\right)\right|{/tex}
17.

The number of feasible solutions of the linear programming problem given as
Maximize z = 15x + 30y subject to constraints:
3x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 12, x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 10, x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 is

a. 1
b. 2
c. 3
d. infinite
18.

The feasible region of a linear programming problem is shown in the figure below:

Which of the following are the possible constraints?

a. x + 2y {tex}\geq{/tex} 4, x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 3, x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
b. x + 2y {tex}\leq{/tex} 4, x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 3, x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
c. x + 2y {tex}\geq{/tex} 4, x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3, x {tex}\geq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0
d. x + 2y {tex}\geq{/tex} 4, x + y {tex}\geq{/tex} 3, x {tex}\leq{/tex} 0, y {tex}\leq{/tex} 0

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions carrying 1 mark each. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below.
19.

Assertion (A): Range of [sin-1 x + 2 cos-1 x] is [0, {tex}\pi{/tex}].
Reason (R): Principal value branch of sin-1 x has range {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
20.

Assertion (A): A line through the points (4, 7, 8) and (2, 3, 4) is parallel to a line through the points (-1, -2, 1) and (1, 2, 5).
Reason (R): Lines {tex}\vec {\text r }{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {\text r }{/tex} = {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}+\mu \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} are parallel if {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1} \cdot \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} = 0.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.

SECTION - B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.

If {tex}\vec {\text r}{/tex} = {tex}3 \hat{\text i}-2 \hat{\text j}+6 \hat{\text k}{/tex}, find the value of {tex}(\vec{\text r} \times \hat{\text j}) \cdot(\vec{\text r} \times \hat{\text k}){/tex} - 12.
22.

If the angle between the lines {tex}\frac{\text x-5}{\alpha}=\frac{\text y+2}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z+\frac{24}{5}}{\beta}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x}{1}=\frac{\text y}{0}=\frac{\text z}{1}{/tex} is {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}, find the relation between {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}.
23.

If f(x) = a(tan x - cot x), where a > 0, then find whether f(x) is increasing or decreasing function in its domain.
24.(i)

Evaluate: 3sin-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} + 2 cos-1{tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right){/tex} + cos-1(0)

OR
(ii)

Draw the graph of f(x) = sin-1 x, x {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left[-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]{/tex}. Also, write range of f(x).
25.(i)

If y = {tex}\mathrm{x}^{\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} at x = 1.

OR
(ii)

If x = a sin 2t, y = a(cos 2t + log tan t), then find {tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}.

SECTION-C

This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each.
26.(i)

Find the general solution of the differential equation:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}(xy2) = 2y(1 + x2)

OR
(ii)

Solve the following differential equation:
{tex}\text {xe}^{\frac{\text y}{\text x}}-\text y+\text x \frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0
27.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_1^3 \frac{\sqrt{4-\text x}}{\sqrt{\text x}+\sqrt{4-\text x}} \text {dx}{/tex}
28.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_1^e \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \text x^2-(\text x \log \text x)^2}} \text {dx}{/tex}
29.(i)

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\cos \text x}{\sin 3 \text x} \text {dx}{/tex}

OR
(ii)

Find: {tex}\int{/tex}x2 log (x2 + 1) dx
30.

Determine graphically the minimum value of the following objective function:
z = 500x + 400y
subject to constraints
x + y {tex}\leq{/tex} 200,
x {tex}\geq{/tex} 20,
y {tex}\geq{/tex} 4x,
y {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
31.(i)

A pair of dice is thrown simultaneously. If X denotes the absolute difference of numbers obtained on the pair of dice, then find the probability distribution of X.

OR
(ii)

There are two coins. One of them is a biased coin such that P (head): P (tail) is 1 : 3 and the other coin is a fair coin. A coin is selected at random and tossed once. If the coin showed head, then find the probability that it is a biased coin.

SECTION-D

This section comprises long answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each.
32.

Show that a function f: {tex}\mathbb R \rightarrow \mathbb R{/tex} defined as f(x) = {tex}\frac{5 \text x-3}{4}{/tex} is both one-one and onto.
33.

The area of the region bounded by the line y = mx (m > 0), the curve x2 + y2 = 4 and the x-axis in the first quadrant is {tex}\frac \pi2{/tex} units. Using integration, find the value of m.
34.(i)

If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0&2 \\ 0&2&1 \\ 2&0&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, prove that A3 - 6A2 + 7A + 2I = 0.

OR
(ii)

If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 2 \\ 5 & -7 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find A-1 and use it to solve the following system of equations:
3x + 5y = 11, 2x - 7y = -3.
35.(i)

Find the value of b so that the lines {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y-\text b}{3}=\frac{\text z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x-4}{5}=\frac{\text y-1}{2}{/tex} = z are intersecting lines. Also, find the point of intersection of these given lines.

OR
(ii)

Find the equations of all the sides of the parallelogram ABCD whose vertices are A(4, 7, 8), B(2, 3, 4), C(-1, -2, 1) and D(1, 2, 5). Also, find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular from A to CD.

SECTION-E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study-1
36.

An octagonal prism is a three-dimensional polyhedron bounded by two octagonal bases and eight rectangular side faces. It has 24 edges and 16 vertices.

The prism is rolled along the rectangular faces and number on the bottom face (touching the ground) is noted. Let X denote the number obtained on the bottom face and the following table give the probability distribution of X.

X: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
P(X): p 2p 2p p 2p p2 2p2 7p2 + p

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i)

Find the value of p.

 1
(ii)

Find P(X > 6).

 1
(iii)(a)

Find P(X = 3m), where m is a natural number.

 2

OR
(iii)(b)

Find the mean E(X).

 2

Case Study-2
37.

In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m3. The cost of land is ₹ 5,000 per square metre and cost of digging increases with depth and for the whole tank, it is ₹ 40,000 h2, where h is the depth of the tank in metres. x is the side of the square base of the tank in metres.
ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i)

Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x.

 1
(ii)

Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}.

 1
(iii)(a)

Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum.

 2

OR
(iii)(b)

Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0.

 2

Case Study-3
38.

A volleyball player serves the ball which takes a parabolic path given by the equation h(t) = {tex}-\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{t}^2+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{t}+1{/tex}, where h(t) is the height of ball at any time t (in seconds), (t {tex}\geq{/tex} 0).

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

(i)

Is h(t) a continuous function? Justify.

 2
(ii)

Find the time at which the height of the ball is maximum.

 2

Correct Answer: (b) {tex}\frac {\pi}{6}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex} A+A^{\prime}=\sqrt{3} I {/tex}
{tex} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos x & \sin x \\ -\sin x & \cos x \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} \sin x & -\sin x \\ \sin x & \cos x \end{array}\right] {/tex}
{tex} A+A^{\prime}=\sqrt{3} I \text {. } {/tex}
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos x & \sin x \\ -\sin x & \cos x \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos x & -\sin x \\ \sin x & \cos x \end{array}\right]=\sqrt{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos x+\cos x & 0 \\ 0 & \cos x+\cos x \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{3} \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 \cos x & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \cos x \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sqrt{3} & 0 \\ 0 & \sqrt{3} \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos x=\sqrt{3} . {/tex}
{tex} \cos x=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {/tex}
{tex} \cos x=\cos \frac{\pi}{6} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}

Show that a function f: {tex}\mathbb R \rightarrow \mathbb R{/tex} defined as f(x) = {tex}\frac{5 \text x-3}{4}{/tex} is both one-one and onto.

Solution:

Let x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} R (Domain) such that
f(x1) = f(x2)

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x1 = 5x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = x2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one-one.
To show f is onto:
Let y {tex}\in{/tex} R (co-domain).
Then f(x) = y for some x.
if, y = {tex}\frac{5 x-3}{4}{/tex}
4y = 5x-3
x = {tex}\frac{4 y+3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R (Domain)
Thus, for every y {tex}\in{/tex} R (co-domain), there exists a pre-image x= {tex}\frac{4 y+3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} R (domain) such that {tex}f\left(\frac{4 y+3}{5}\right){/tex} = y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Range of f(x) = R = codomain of f(x).
Hence, f is onto.

= {tex}\frac{-1}{31}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -7 & -5 \\ -2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 11 \\ -3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Solution:

As lines are intersecting, {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \cdot\left(\left(\overrightarrow{b_1} \times \overrightarrow{b_2}\right)\right.{/tex} = 0.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccr} 3 & 1-b & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2
Any point on the line {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y-2}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-3}{4}{/tex} is
({tex}2 \lambda+1{/tex}, {tex}3 \lambda+2{/tex}, {tex}4 \lambda+3{/tex}), {tex}\lambda \in \mathrm{R}{/tex}
For the point of intersection, this point must lie on the line
{tex}\frac{\text x-4}{5}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{2}{/tex} = z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 \lambda+1-4}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda+2-1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}4 \lambda{/tex} + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -1

Solution:

Equation of the line AB: {tex}\frac{\text x-4}{2}=\frac{\text y-7}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-8}{4}{/tex}
Equation of the line BC: {tex}\frac{\text x-2}{3}=\frac{\text y-3}{5}=\frac{\text z-4}{3}{/tex}
Equation of the line CD: {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+2}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}{/tex}
Equation of the line DA: {tex}\frac{\text x-4}{3}=\frac{\text y-7}{5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-8}{3}{/tex}
Let P be foot of perpendicular from A to CD.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinates of P are ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 1, {tex}2\lambda{/tex} - 2, {tex}2\lambda{/tex} + 1) for some {tex}\lambda{/tex}
d.r.'s of AP are ({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 5, {tex}2\lambda{/tex} - 9, {tex}2\lambda{/tex} - 7)
since AP {tex}\perp{/tex} CD
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1({tex}\lambda{/tex} - 5) + 2({tex}2 \lambda{/tex} - 9) + 2({tex}2 \lambda{/tex} - 7) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}9\lambda{/tex} = 37 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{37}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordinates of P are {tex}\left(\frac{28}{9}, \frac{56}{9}, \frac{83}{9}\right){/tex}

Case Study-1
36.

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
An octagonal prism is a three-dimensional polyhedron bounded by two octagonal bases and eight rectangular side faces. It has 24 edges and 16 vertices.

The prism is rolled along the rectangular faces and number on the bottom face (touching the ground) is noted. Let X denote the number obtained on the bottom face and the following table give the probability distribution of X.

X: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
P(X): p 2p 2p p 2p p2 2p2 7p2 + p
  1. Find the value of p. (1)
  2. Find P(X > 6). (1)
  3. Find P(X = 3m), where m is a natural number. (2)
    OR
    Find the mean E(X). (2)
Solution:

  1. 10p2 + 9p = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = {tex}\frac {1}{10}{/tex}

  2. P(X > 6) = 9p2 + p
    = {tex}\frac{9}{100}+\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{19}{100}{/tex}

  3. P(X = 3 m) = P(3) + P(6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2p + p2 = {tex}\frac{21}{100}{/tex}
    OR
    E(X) = {tex}\Sigma{/tex}XP(X) = p + 4p + 6p + 4p + 10p+ 6p2 + 14p2 + 56p2 + 8p
    = 33p + 76p2
    = {tex}\frac{406}{100} \text { or } \frac{203}{50}{/tex}

37.

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m3. ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM

  1. Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum. (2)
    OR
    Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0. (2)
Solution:

  1. C = 40000h2 + 5000x2
    as x2h = 250
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x2

  2. {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x

  3. For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
    showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10
    OR
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex}) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10).
    Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0

Case Study-3

A volleyball player serves the ball which takes a parabolic path given by the equation h(t) = {tex}-\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{t}^2+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{t}+1{/tex}, where h(t) is the height of ball at any time t (in seconds), (t {tex}\geq{/tex} 0).

Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Is h(t) a continuous function? Justify. (2)
  2. Find the time at which the height of the ball is maximum. (2)
Solution:
  1. h(t) = {tex}-\frac{7}{2} t^2+\frac{13}{2} t+1{/tex}
    Clearly h(t) is a polynomial function, hence continuous.
    Hence h(t) is a continuous function.
  2. For maximum height,
    {tex}\frac{d h}{d t}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -7t + {tex}\frac{13}{2}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{13}{14}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text h}{\text {dt}^2}{/tex} = -7 < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} height is maximum at t = {tex}\frac{13}{14}{/tex}
